current_executable_status: executable (code_ref swm.world_model_v2.registry.families.interaction:turnout_probability resolves; test_ref tests/test_interaction_families.py present); no validation record, no parameter pack

primary_sources.title: A Theory of the Calculus of Voting
primary_sources.authors: William H. Riker; Peter C. Ordeshook
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Formal expected-utility model of the decision to vote (structural). Introduces the D term (citizen duty) to rescue rationality of voting.

primary_sources.title: What Affects Voter Turnout? (review)
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Review of empirical turnout determinants and the descriptive-fit limits of rational-choice/calculus-of-voting (the 'paradox of voting'). DOI/venue not re-fetched; cite as review context only.

formal_model: Calculus of voting: R = P*B - C + D, vote iff R>0, where P=prob the ballot is pivotal, B=candidate differential benefit, C=cost of voting, D=expressive/duty benefit. Registry implementation is a bounded-logit surrogate: p = sigmoid(logit(base_rate) - k_c*cost + k_b*benefit + k_d*duty + k_m*mobilized).
causal_assumptions: Expected-utility rationality of the voter
Voter perceives (or acts as if perceiving) pivotality P; in large electorates P is near-zero so D must dominate to explain positive turnout
Additive separability of cost/benefit/duty/mobilization terms on the turnout logit
Coefficients are electorate-specific and must be refit (labeled priors, not identified from any committed dataset)

reported_coefficients.name: k_cost (in-code prior)
reported_coefficients.context: Hard-coded labeled prior on the turnout logit, NOT an empirical estimate

reported_coefficients.name: k_benefit (in-code prior)
reported_coefficients.context: Hard-coded labeled prior; unfitted

reported_coefficients.name: k_duty (in-code prior)
reported_coefficients.context: Hard-coded labeled prior; unfitted

reported_coefficients.name: k_mobilized (in-code prior)
reported_coefficients.context: Hard-coded labeled prior; unfitted

replication_evidence: Formal model canonical and pervasively cited; however the calculus-of-voting has a well-documented descriptive-fit failure (the paradox of voting: P~0 in mass electorates makes P*B negligible, so the model cannot explain observed turnout without a large unmodeled D). It is a structural scaffold, not a calibrated predictor.
critiques_or_nulls: Paradox of voting: instrumental term P*B ~ 0 in large electorates; positive turnout requires the D (duty) term to carry nearly all explanatory weight
In-code coefficients (1.0/0.8/1.2/0.6) are arbitrary priors with no empirical provenance
applicability_limits: Requires an externally supplied base_rate; not self-calibrating
transport_risks: Coefficients not identified from data; any cross-electorate transfer is uncalibrated
local_refit_feasible: partial - structural form is executable, but no turnout micro-dataset is committed on disk; fitting k_* requires importing CPS/ANES or a GOTV experimental voter file
best_validation_route: Seed k_* from a turnout micro-dataset or GOTV replication; posterior-predictive check against held-out electorate turnout; explicitly document paradox-of-voting miscalibration as a known failure mode
expected_applicability: medium (broad conceptual scope but weak calibrated predictive power)
planned_implementation: Keep the bounded-logit surrogate; add a duty-dominant parameterization; attach an empirically-anchored duty/mobilization prior sourced from the mobilization and social_pressure packs below
planned_parameter_packs: calculus_of_voting_priors (labeled reference-class; blocked from production until fit)
promotion_blocker: No empirical validation record, no parameter pack, coefficients are unfitted priors, and the mechanism has a known descriptive-fit failure (paradox of voting). Formal model verified; numbers are priors not estimates.

current_executable_status: executable (shares code_ref turnout_probability, mobilized term; test_ref present); no validation record, no pack

primary_sources.title: The Effects of Canvassing, Telephone Calls, and Direct Mail on Voter Turnout: A Field Experiment
primary_sources.authors: Alan S. Gerber; Donald P. Green
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Foundational randomized GOTV field experiment (New Haven 1998, ~30,000 voters): face-to-face canvassing raises turnout substantially, direct mail slightly, commercial phone calls not at all.

primary_sources.title: Get Out the Vote: How to Increase Voter Turnout (book, meta-estimates)
primary_sources.authors: Donald P. Green; Alan S. Gerber
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Meta-synthesis rules of thumb across hundreds of GOTV experiments (canvassing ~1 added vote per ~14 contacts; commercial phone ~0; mail small). Book-level; page figures not independently re-fetched.

formal_model: Mobilized contact adds a positive shift to the turnout logit: p_treated = sigmoid(logit(baseline) + k_m*mobilized). Empirically summarized as an intent-to-treat (ITT) turnout difference and a complier average causal effect (CACE) via IV on contact.
causal_assumptions: Randomized assignment of contact attempt (ITT identified by design)
CACE identification for door-to-door requires exclusion restriction (assignment affects turnout only through contact) and monotonicity
SUTVA / limited spillover between treated and control households
Validated turnout outcome from voter file (not self-report)

reported_coefficients.name: Face-to-face canvassing turnout (New Haven 1998)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: control ~44% -> canvassed ~53% (~+9pp raw ITT among reached)
reported_coefficients.context: Raw/ITT-style comparison; per-contact CACE is larger after IV correction for contact rate. SE not extracted from primary table.

reported_coefficients.name: Commercial phone-bank effect on turnout
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Gerber & Green 2000; corrected in Gerber & Green 2005 reply to Imai
reported_coefficients.context: Null survives the Imai (2005) merge-error correction

reported_coefficients.name: Direct mail effect on turnout
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: small positive (~fraction of a point per piece)
reported_coefficients.context: Slight positive; magnitude modest

reported_coefficients.name: GOTV canvassing rule-of-thumb (book meta)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: ~1 additional vote per ~14 door-to-door contacts (~7pp per contact)
reported_coefficients.source_ref: Green & Gerber, Get Out the Vote (book)
reported_coefficients.context: Meta rule of thumb; not re-fetched at page level

datasets_in_literature: Green, Gerber & Nickerson JOP 2003 six-city local-election experiments
replication_evidence: Extensively replicated across hundreds of experiments; door-to-door canvassing robustly positive, commercial phone weak/null. Imai (2005, APSR) caught a data-merge/coding error in Gerber & Green (2000); Gerber & Green (2005) correction shows the substantive phone-null is unchanged after repair.
critiques_or_nulls: Imai (2005) reanalysis (merge error + IV/matching dispute); resolved by Gerber-Green (2005) correction with null phone effect preserved
Effect sizes are electorate/context specific; canvassing effects shrink in high-salience elections and with low contact rates
applicability_limits: Effects depend on baseline turnout and contact rate
transport_risks: Magnitude varies by election salience, electorate, and canvasser quality; US-centric evidence base
local_refit_feasible: no - no GOTV experimental dataset is committed on disk (Upworthy on-disk data is content A/B, not turnout); packs must be seeded from published_research and refit needs an external replication voter file
best_validation_route: Attach published_research packs (canvassing +~7pp/contact, commercial phone ~0, mail ~+0.5pp) with broad transport uncertainty; hold out a distinct replication (e.g., Green-Gerber-Nickerson 2003) to test transfer
planned_implementation: Bind mobilized term to a published_research pack keyed by contact_type; expose contact_rate so ITT vs CACE is explicit
planned_parameter_packs: gotv_canvassing (ATE ~+7pp per reached contact, wide CI)
promotion_blocker: No in-model validation record and no committed dataset for local refit; packs are transferable published_research priors only until a held-out replication passes.

current_registry_status: not_registered (no dedicated family; conceptually a mobilization sub-mechanism / observability shifter on the turnout logit)
current_executable_status: not_executable (no dedicated code_ref); could be realized as a mobilization parameter pack or a new social_pressure family

primary_sources.title: Social Pressure and Voter Turnout: Evidence from a Large-Scale Field Experiment
primary_sources.authors: Alan S. Gerber; Donald P. Green; Christopher W. Larimer
primary_sources.what_it_provides: The 'Neighbors' mailer experiment (Michigan Aug 2006 primary). Four escalating social-pressure mail treatments plus control; validated turnout outcome; the largest known direct-mail turnout effect.

formal_model: Additive social-pressure term on the turnout logit whose magnitude increases in the observability/publicizing of the individual's vote: Δturnout monotone in pressure intensity (Civic Duty < Hawthorne < Self < Neighbors). Equivalent to a large positive shock to the D/mobilization term in the calculus-of-voting surrogate.
causal_assumptions: Household-level randomization of mailer arm (design-identified ATE)
Validated turnout from state voter file
SUTVA within household treated as unit (within-household spillover expected and part of the estimand)
No differential attrition across arms

reported_coefficients.name: Civic Duty mailer effect
reported_coefficients.uncertainty_or_null: SE ~0.3pp (design-derived, NOT extracted from primary Table 3)
reported_coefficients.context: 'Do Your Civic Duty' mailer vs control

reported_coefficients.name: Hawthorne mailer effect
reported_coefficients.context: 'You are being studied' mailer vs control

reported_coefficients.name: Self mailer effect
reported_coefficients.context: Shows household's own past vote record

reported_coefficients.name: Neighbors mailer effect
reported_coefficients.context: Shows neighbors' past vote records; largest arm

reported_coefficients.name: Control baseline turnout
reported_coefficients.context: Widely cited control turnout; NOT re-confirmed from primary text here

reported_coefficients.name: Sample size
reported_coefficients.context: Household count (~180,000) verified via secondary summaries; individual count (~344,084) recalled, not re-verified

replication_evidence: Robustly replicated: Gerber, Green & Larimer (2010) pride/shame follow-up; Mann (2010) backlash study; Panagopoulos and others confirm monotone social-pressure gradient. Effect ranking (Neighbors > Self > Hawthorne > Civic Duty) is highly stable.
critiques_or_nulls: Some backlash/annoyance (Mann 2010) and ethical concerns about shaming
Exact standard errors were NOT extracted from the primary Table 3 in this pass (source PDF returned as binary); SEs above are design-derived approximations flagged verified:false
Control baseline (29.7%) and individual N (344,084) not primary-confirmed here
applicability_limits: Low-salience elections where mail can move turnout most
Requires publicizable vote records (US validated-vote norm)
transport_risks: Depends on cultural acceptability of publicizing turnout and on a public voter file; magnitude context-specific
local_refit_feasible: no on-disk data; effect sizes enter as a published_research pack from GGL 2008
best_validation_route: Add a social_pressure family (or mobilization sub-pack) with the four-arm gradient; given strong replication, transfer-test the ordering/magnitude across electorates
expected_applicability: high (best-identified single causal effect in this cluster)
planned_implementation: New social_pressure family OR mobilization pack: turnout logit += g(pressure_intensity), calibrated to +1.8/+2.5/+4.9/+8.1pp arms
planned_parameter_packs: social_pressure_mail (Civic Duty +1.8 / Hawthorne +2.5 / Self +4.9 / Neighbors +8.1 pp)
promotion_blocker: No executable registry family yet; needs family + pack. Point estimates verified; SEs and baseline not extracted from the primary table (flagged verified:false).

current_registry_status: not_registered (no dedicated family; would combine a direct-treatment term with a peer-contagion term)

primary_sources.title: A 61-million-person experiment in social influence and political mobilization
primary_sources.authors: Robert M. Bond; Christopher J. Fariss; Jason J. Jones; Adam D. I. Kramer; Cameron Marlow; Jaime E. Settle; James H. Fowler
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Randomized Facebook GOTV experiment (2010 US midterms, ~61M users): direct effect of a social message vs informational vs control on validated turnout, self-expression, information-seeking, and close-friend social contagion.

formal_model: Two-component mobilization: (1) direct treatment effect of the social message on ego's turnout; (2) peer-contagion term where ego's turnout probability rises with the number of treated close friends. Contagion concentrated in strong (face-to-face) ties.
causal_assumptions: Randomized assignment of social vs informational vs (small) control message
Validated turnout via matched public voter records (not self-report)
Contagion identified by comparing friends of treated vs friends of control egos; assumes tie-strength proxies (interaction frequency) capture influence channel
No large unmodeled confounding of friendship and co-turnout beyond controls

reported_coefficients.name: Direct effect: social message on validated turnout
reported_coefficients.context: Social message (friend faces) vs control on real (validated) voting

reported_coefficients.name: Effect on 'I Voted' self-expression
reported_coefficients.context: Self-expression / button click

reported_coefficients.name: Effect on polling-place information seeking
reported_coefficients.context: Clicking the polling-place locator

reported_coefficients.name: Close-friend contagion on validated turnout
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: +0.224pp per close friend who received the social message
reported_coefficients.context: Per-treated-close-friend increase in ego's real voting; ordinary (weak-tie) friends showed ~no transmission

reported_coefficients.name: Total additional votes generated
reported_coefficients.context: Contagion effect exceeded the direct effect

datasets_in_literature: Facebook 2010 user activity logs matched to validated public voter records (proprietary; not publicly redistributable)
replication_evidence: Jones et al. (2017, PLOS ONE) re-ran a randomized social-mobilization experiment in the 2012 US presidential election with consistent (further) evidence. Bond 2012 itself is a single-platform, single-election result.
critiques_or_nulls: Per-user effects are tiny in absolute terms; societal impact is driven by scale (60M users)
External validity to non-Facebook platforms/elections unestablished
applicability_limits: Large online social platforms with observable friend networks
transport_risks: Single platform (2010 Facebook), single US election
Effects likely differ under different feed designs and message-fatigue regimes
local_refit_feasible: no - proprietary platform data unavailable; enters only as a published_research pack with an explicit platform-transport caveat
best_validation_route: Keep as a published_research pack (direct +0.39pp; per-close-friend +0.224pp) with a strong platform/era transport warning; validate only that model spillover magnitudes stay within this envelope
planned_implementation: Optional network-mobilization pack layering a direct-message term and a peer-contagion term (scaled by count of treated strong ties) on the turnout family
promotion_blocker: No executable family and no accessible dataset; single-platform/single-election provenance gives high transport risk. All extracted numbers verified.

family: Donation / charitable giving response to an ask
current_executable_status: executable (code_ref swm.world_model_v2.registry.families.interaction:donation_amount resolves; test_ref present); no validation record, no pack

primary_sources.title: Does Price Matter in Charitable Giving? Evidence from a Large-Scale Natural Field Experiment
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Randomized direct-mail experiment (>50,000 prior donors): presence of a matching grant, match ratio (1:1/2:1/3:1), match cap, and suggested-amount, on probability of donating and revenue per solicitation.

primary_sources.title: Testing for Altruism and Social Pressure in Charitable Giving
primary_sources.authors: Stefano DellaVigna; John A. List; Ulrike Malmendier
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Door-to-door fundraising with pre-announcement flyers (opt-out option) that separates altruism from social pressure; structural estimate of the social-pressure cost of declining a solicitor.

formal_model: Bounded donation response: give-probability and amount increase in ask/match-generosity/affinity/history and saturate at capacity. Structural decomposition (DLM): utility of giving = altruism/warm-glow term minus a social-pressure cost S incurred when declining a face-to-face ask; observed giving = argmax over give/decline including S.
causal_assumptions: Randomized treatment (match presence/ratio; flyer/opt-out)
Karlan-List: match manipulations exogenous to donor propensity
DLM: flyer timing randomizes ability to sort into/out of the solicitation; structural identification of S from opt-out take-up and giving shifts
Donations observed (revealed behavior, not self-report)

reported_coefficients.name: Match offer on probability of donating
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: +~22% (relative increase in P(donate) vs no-match control)
reported_coefficients.context: Announcing a match raises response rate

reported_coefficients.name: Match offer on revenue per solicitation
reported_coefficients.context: Amount raised per letter

reported_coefficients.name: Match ratio (2:1, 3:1 vs 1:1)
reported_coefficients.context: Larger match ratios do NOT beat 1:1 (price-insensitivity)

reported_coefficients.name: Social-pressure cost of saying no (in-person ask)
reported_coefficients.context: Structural estimate of utility cost of declining a door-to-door solicitor

reported_coefficients.name: Opt-out flyer on door-opening
reported_coefficients.context: Pre-announcement lets households avoid the ask

reported_coefficients.name: 'Do Not Disturb' box on giving
reported_coefficients.context: Removing social pressure lowers small, pressure-driven gifts

datasets_in_literature: Karlan-List nonprofit direct-mail donor file (>50,000 prior donors)
DLM door-to-door fundraising field data (charity solicitation with flyer randomization)
replication_evidence: Match-effect and ratio-insensitivity broadly consistent with subsequent giving experiments; social-pressure mechanism corroborated by related door-to-door and 'avoid-the-ask' studies (e.g., DellaVigna-List-Malmendier-Rao on voting-to-tell-others). Small-match literature (e.g., Karlan-List-Shafir) refines magnitudes.
critiques_or_nulls: Match generosity is nearly price-insensitive (2:1/3:1 no better than 1:1) - a null on the intensive price margin
Door-to-door welfare can be negative (DLM): much giving is pressure-driven, not altruistic
Registry's donation_amount coefficients are labeled priors, not fit to these studies
applicability_limits: Solicited giving (mail/door) with a defined ask
Effects specific to prior-donor lists (Karlan-List) and cold door-to-door (DLM)
transport_risks: Match/pressure magnitudes vary by cause, donor relationship, and channel; dollar-valued pressure cost is US-context specific
local_refit_feasible: no - no donation dataset is committed on disk (Upworthy is content A/B, not giving); packs seed from Karlan-List / DLM published_research
best_validation_route: Attach match-offer and social-pressure packs to donation_response; validate on an external fundraising replication as held-out before any local claim
planned_implementation: Bind donation_amount/ P(donate) to packs: match-presence multiplier (+~19% revenue, +~22% response, ratio-insensitive) and a social-pressure cost term S ($3.5/$1.4) for avoidable-vs-unavoidable asks
planned_parameter_packs: door_to_door_social_pressure (S=$3.5 in-state / $1.4 out-of-state; -30% giving when avoidable)
promotion_blocker: No committed donation dataset for local refit and no in-model validation record; packs are transferable published_research priors until a held-out fundraising replication passes.

family: Political persuasion (campaign contact on vote choice)
current_registry_status: not_registered (NONE explicit; important as a near-null guardrail prior, distinct from mobilization which moves turnout not choice)

primary_sources.title: The Minimal Persuasive Effects of Campaign Contact in General Elections: Evidence from 49 Field Experiments
primary_sources.authors: Joshua L. Kalla; David E. Broockman
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Meta-analysis of 40 field experiments + 9 original experiments (49 total) on campaign contact/advertising and candidate choice in general elections: best estimate of the average persuasive effect is ZERO.

primary_sources.title: Persuasion: Empirical Evidence
primary_sources.what_it_provides: Survey/framework for persuasion effects across consumers/voters/donors/investors; defines the persuasion-rate metric and reviews media-effects magnitudes (generally modest, context-dependent).

formal_model: Persuasion-rate framing (DellaVigna-Gentzkow): fraction of the exposed population whose behavior shifts, f = (change in behavior) / (exposure * (persuadable share)). Estimand here is the ATE of campaign contact on candidate choice / vote share; Kalla-Broockman best estimate ~0 in general elections, with rare nonzero regimes.
causal_assumptions: Randomized contact/advertising assignment across the pooled experiments
Outcome = candidate choice / vote intention or vote share (persuasion), NOT turnout (mobilization)
Meta-analytic pooling assumes comparable estimands; publication-bias corrections applied by authors
General-election context (competitive, well-known candidates, late campaign)
temporal_scale: event (campaign cycle); persuasion effects that do appear tend to decay

reported_coefficients.name: Average persuasive effect of campaign contact (general elections)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: ~0 (best estimate zero) across 49 experiments (40 meta + 9 original)
reported_coefficients.uncertainty_or_null: tightly bounded near zero; authors' central claim
reported_coefficients.context: Applies to persuasion (candidate choice) in general elections, not turnout

reported_coefficients.name: Nonzero-persuasion regimes (exceptions)
reported_coefficients.value_or_null: detectable only (a) when candidates take unusually unpopular positions AND campaigns invest heavily in identifying persuadables, or (b) when contact occurs long before election day - and that early effect decays
reported_coefficients.context: Boundary conditions where persuasion is not zero

replication_evidence: The near-zero general-election persuasion finding is corroborated by Broockman & Kalla's later work (e.g., 2020 US presidential campaign field experiments, AJPS 2023) showing small/decaying persuasion. Robust and much-cited.
critiques_or_nulls: THIS IS THE KEY NULL: do NOT encode large persuasion effects. Average general-election persuasion from campaign contact is best estimated at zero.
Persuasion CAN be nonzero for ballot measures, primaries, early contact, and unusually unpopular positions - so a flat zero is a prior, not a law
Distinguish sharply from mobilization (which has real positive effects on turnout)
applicability_limits: General-election candidate persuasion via direct contact/ads
Does not govern turnout, ballot-measure, or primary persuasion, which can differ
transport_risks: Zero-average is regime-specific (competitive general elections, late campaign); early-campaign and low-information contexts can show transient effects
local_refit_feasible: no - meta-analytic near-null; encode as a prior/guardrail rather than a fitted coefficient
best_validation_route: Encode as a near-zero persuasion prior with wide-but-zero-centered uncertainty; validate that the world model does NOT over-attribute vote-choice change to campaign contact in general-election scenarios
expected_applicability: medium-high as a guardrail (prevents over-estimating persuasion)
planned_implementation: Add a persuasion guardrail: default general-election contact->vote-choice effect ~0 with documented exception regimes; never a large default
planned_parameter_packs: persuasion_exception_regimes (small, decaying; unpopular-position + heavy targeting, or early contact)
promotion_blocker: No executable family; and the credible 'estimate' is a near-zero prior with strong context-dependence, so it serves as a guardrail rather than a positive mechanism. Central null verified.
